behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions with descriptive error messages.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser validation/error-formatting logic in one component. No real OS/network/subprocess, no model output, no multi-step agent chain. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text is only about syntactic/range validation (e.g., month 13, day 32, malformed syntax), which is fully reproducible in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Call parser with invalid cron strings and assert structured error type/message includes the offending field/token and reason.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard and edge cron semantics (last day of month, nearest weekday).
behaviors.justification: Core failure is deterministic calendar arithmetic/cron semantics in the calculator. No required real infrastructure, no AI dependency, no multi-agent sequencing. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text targets correctness of timestamp computation itself, not module handoff or external runtime effects.
behaviors.test_description: Given fixed start instants and cron expressions (including L, W semantics), assert next N timestamps exactly match expected UTC/local datetimes.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector flags schedules firing within a configured minimum gap and reports conflicting windows.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is at module handoff: schedule expansion/next-run generation feeding overlap-window computation and reporting. Unit tests of comparator logic alone can miss mismatches in timestamp normalization/window construction across modules. Integration-brake check: no real OS/network/subprocess (so not System), no AI/ML output dependency (not Agentic), no multi-agent chain (not Workflow).
behaviors.test_description: Wire parser + run-time generator + overlap detector; feed two cron schedules and a 5-minute gap, then assert detected conflict windows and boundaries are correct.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling handles DST spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity for a 2:30 AM schedule.
behaviors.justification: Failure typically appears where scheduler logic interacts with timezone/DST rule application (local-time interpretation, nonexistent/ambiguous times). Mocked timezone conversion can hide real DST semantics, so one-function unit isolation is too low. Integration-brake check: no need for real network/subprocess/OS primitives beyond in-process timezone library rules (so not System); no AI dependency; no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Run scheduler with a real timezone ruleset (e.g., America/New_York) across known DST transition dates and assert expected handling for nonexistent 2:30 AM (spring-forward) and duplicated 2:30 AM (fall-back).

behaviors.description: When next run is in the past after downtime, engine applies missed-run policy (skip vs backfill) correctly.
behaviors.justification: Behavior spans computed schedule state + current-time comparison + policy module decision, so real failures occur in orchestration/state handoff, not only a single pure function. Integration-brake check: no required real OS/network/subprocess, no AI/ML dependency, no multi-agent sequence. Higher levels were considered but rejected because behavior text is policy orchestration inside the scheduling engine.
behaviors.test_description: Wire scheduler state + next-run calculator + missed-run policy evaluator; simulate downtime where next run is stale and assert resulting action (skip or queued backfill) matches policy.
